purpose: Fiscal 2020 Adopted Expense Budget Adjustment Summary Page 71 The Council’s food initiatives support critical programs that assist low -income New Yorkers to access food and federal benefits, as well as increase awareness of healthy food options and nutrition. This funding supports hundreds of soup kitchens and food pantries across the City that supply freshly cooked meals, fresh meat and produce, shelf-stable grocery items, and frozen food to families and individuals. Food initiative funding also supports programs that improve access to affordable, healthy food in low -income communities through farmers markets, community gardens, urban agriculture, nutritional education, and hands -on training such as cook ing demonstrations and health-focused classes. Council funding also supports the Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC) Tax Assistance Program, which provides free tax assistance services to low-income, working individuals and families, and Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) education, eligibility screening, and application and recertification support. This f iscal year, the Council enhances the Access to Healthy Food and Nutritional Education initiative by $1.2 million, for a total of $ 2.26 million. Of the $1.2 million, $1 million supports a pilot program at the City University of New York (CUNY) to increase food access for food insecure CUNY students. The remaining enhanced funding supports citywide and community -based organizations to improve food a ccess and nutrition education in low -income communities. Additionally, the Council enhances the Food Pantries initiative by $1 million f or a total of $5.66 million in Fiscal 2020. Finally, the Council successfully negotiated with the Administration to incl ude one-time funding of $1 million for Fiscal 2020 to sup port Halal and Kosher meals at Department of Education’s (DOE) schools. This initiative was funded by the Council in Fiscal 2019. Fiscal 2020 Food Initiatives

purpose: Fiscal 2020 Adopted Expense Budget Adjustment Summary Page 119 In the Fiscal 2020 Adopted Budget, the City Council will provide $4.6 million towards a variety of public safety initiatives and programs that support the Council’s efforts towards the provision of public safety and violence reduction across the City. The Council continues its commitment to Art a Catalyst for Change to use arts and education for violence reduction and conflict mediation. The Council also continues the partnership with the Administration in funding the Crisis Management System, which uses a community-based approach to prevent gun violence, restore damage done to communities, and respond to specific incidents. The total funding for the Crisis Management System in Fiscal 2020 is $36.2 million, of which the Administration provides $33.3 million and the Council provides $2.9 million. New in Fiscal 2020 is $1 million for the Hate Crimes Prevention initiative, which support s community-based organizations to provide hate crime prevention services, education, and healing programs to bring communities closer together and counteract the serious and negative consequences brought on by bias and hate incidents. Services will focus on the diverse communities of New York City and will include training on bystander and upstander interventions, community-based reporting, counseling and peer support, community events, and educational programs. Additionally, pursuant to Local Law 46, the Administration will provide $710,000 to fund the newly created Office of Hate Crime Prevention. The Council successfully lobbied for the Administration to include $6.9 million for District Attorneys programs and operational needs. The Administration also agreed to continue its commitment to fire safety by allocating $1 million in funding f or the Get Alarmed program. Finally, due to the Council’s support , the Administration will fund $1.5 million to increase the investigator headcount for the Civilian Complaint Review Board. Fiscal 2020 Council Initiatives
